title: Revisit Daily Life in China in 1917 Through Footage Enhanced and Colorized by AI
description:
in Film, Travel |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even for Amer┬şi┬şcans, keep┬şing up with the geopo┬şlit┬şi┬şcal entan┬şgle┬şments of the Uni
body:
in Film, Travel |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even for Amer┬şi┬şcans, keep┬şing up with the geopo┬şlit┬şi┬şcal entan┬şgle┬şments of the Unit┬şed States has nev┬şer been an easy task. More than a cen┬ştu┬şry ago, just a few months after their coun┬ştry got involved in whatÔÇÖs now known as World War I, they got word that the mil┬şi┬ştary of a dis┬ştant nation had joined their side: Chi┬şna, whose image would have been both opaque and for┬şbid┬şding┬şly vast. A dozen years before┬átheyÔÇÖd even heard the name Pearl S. Buck,┬áwhat impres┬şsions of that coun┬ştry they had would have come from scat┬ştered sources like post-Opi┬şum Wars mis┬şsion┬şary pub┬şli┬şca┬ştions, news┬şpa┬şper cov┬şer┬şage of com┬şpli┬şcat┬şed events like┬áthe Box┬şer Rebel┬şlion and the fall of the Qing dynasty, and silent-film genre stereo┬ştypes. (Per┬şhaps the rare read┬şer got ahold of John Thom┬şsonÔÇÖs Through Chi┬şna with a Cam┬şera.) Most could live a life┬ştime with┬şout a glimpse of ÔÇťthe real Chi┬şna.ÔÇŁ
By the end of 1917, how┬şev┬şer, ÔÇťthere were at least 10 doc┬şu┬şmen┬ştaries avail┬şable to sat┬şis┬şfy curios┬şi┬şty about AmericaÔÇÖs new ally in the Far East,ÔÇŁ accord┬şing to the Nation┬şal Film Preser┬şva┬ştion Foun┬şda┬ştion. Most were shorts that played along┬şside fea┬ştures, but A Trip Through Chi┬şna was dif┬şfer┬şent. At least five years in the mak┬şing, ÔÇťthe doc┬şu┬şmen┬ştary was the brain┬şchild of Ben┬şjamin Brod┬şsky, a wide┬şly trav┬şeled Russ┬şian-born busi┬şness┬şman who claimed to speak 11 lan┬şguages. Accord┬şing to a 1912 Mov┬şing Pic┬şture World pro┬şfile, the young entre┬şpre┬şneur had moved to Chi┬şna from San Fran┬şcis┬şco after the 1906 Earth┬şquake and set up shop as a film exhibitor. Soon, as the Amer┬şi┬şcan rep┬şre┬şsen┬şta┬ştive of Vari┬şety Film Exchange, he had a hand in dis┬ştri┬şb┬şu┬ştion and by 1909 branched into film pro┬şduc┬ştion in Shang┬şhai and Hong Kong. While jug┬şgling busi┬şness inter┬şests, he filmed his trav┬şels,ÔÇŁ all of which took place not just before Chi┬şnaÔÇÖs eco┬şnom┬şic rise, but┬ábefore even the Com┬şmu┬şnist Rev┬şo┬şlu┬ştion.


	

Brod┬şsky brought 20,000 feet of neg┬şa┬ştives with him back to San Fran┬şcis┬şco, even┬ştu┬şal┬şly cut┬şting it down to ten reels, which would have run around one hour and 50 min┬şutes. Of this fea┬şture-length trav┬şel┬şogue film only cer┬ştain sec┬ştions sur┬şvive, but you can see them enhanced and col┬şorized with arti┬şfi┬şcial intel┬şli┬şgence in the video at the top of the post. (Some of an un-enhanced black-and-white print appears just above.) Bear in mind that col┬şors you see are not, of course, the col┬şors Brod┬şsky would have seen; thereÔÇÖs also some dis┬şcus┬şsion about whether the AI ren┬şdered cer┬ştain com┬şplex┬şions unre┬şal┬şis┬şti┬şcal┬şly dark for the regions in which he shot these scenes. For Chi┬şna is quite a diverse place, not just in region┬şal land┬şscapes, cli┬şmates, and cul┬ştures, but also in the faces of its peo┬şple: some┬şthing many West┬şern┬şers would┬şnÔÇÖt have guessed in the nine┬şteen-tens ÔÇö and for that mat┬şter, some┬şthing a fair few of them donÔÇÖt real┬şize even today.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
Behold the Pho┬ştographs of John Thom┬şson, the First West┬şern Pho┬ştog┬şra┬şph┬şer to Trav┬şel Wide┬şly Through Chi┬şna (1870s)
A Trip Around the World in 1900: See Restored Footage Show┬şing Life in New York, Lon┬şdon, India, Japan, Chi┬şna & Beyond
Footage of Cities Around the World in the 1890s: Lon┬şdon, Tokyo, New York, Venice, Moscow & More
Watch Life on the Streets of Tokyo in Footage Record┬şed in 1913: Caught Between the Tra┬şdi┬ştion┬şal and the Mod┬şern
A Trip Through New York City in 1911: Vin┬ştage Video of NYC Gets Col┬şorized & Revived with Arti┬şfi┬şcial Intel┬şli┬şgence
The Pho┬şto That Trig┬şgered ChinaÔÇÖs Dis┬şas┬ştrous Cul┬ştur┬şal Rev┬şo┬şlu┬ştion (1966)
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Who Would Be King of the United States If George Washington Had Become a Monarch?
description:
in History |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The young George Wash┬şing┬şton may nev┬şer have hacked up his fatherÔÇÖs cher┬şry tree and refu
body:
in History |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The young George Wash┬şing┬şton may nev┬şer have hacked up his fatherÔÇÖs cher┬şry tree and refused to lie about it, but his life nev┬şer┬şthe┬şless offers plen┬şty of deeds both vir┬ştu┬şous and ade┬şquate┬şly doc┬şu┬şment┬şed. It was no small thing, for instance, to refuse to seek a third term as the first Pres┬şi┬şdent of the Unit┬şed States of Amer┬şi┬şca ÔÇö much less to exchange that title for ÔÇťKing of the Unit┬şed States of Amer┬şi┬şca.ÔÇŁ As every enthu┬şsi┬şast of Amer┬şi┬şcan his┬şto┬şry knows, this set the prece┬şdent, only once vio┬şlat┬şed and there┬şafter writ┬şten into law, of a two-term lim┬şit. But as every enthu┬şsi┬şast of alter┬şnate Amer┬şi┬şcan his┬şto┬şry has won┬şdered, what would have hap┬şpened had Wash┬şing┬şton become king? And if the U.S. monar┬şchy had remained intact for the past 227 years, who would rule it today?
In the Use┬şfulCharts video above, Matt Bak┬şer explains a series of dif┬şfer┬şent suc┬şces┬şsion sce┬şnar┬şios. While none is high┬şly plau┬şsi┬şble in itself, they togeth┬şer give an idea of the lines along which Amer┬şi┬şcan monar┬şchi┬şcal his┬şto┬şry could have played out, at least assum┬şing that every oth┬şer event played out exact┬şly the same way as it has in our real┬şi┬şty.
One of the first com┬şpli┬şcat┬şing fac┬ştors is that Wash┬şing┬şton him┬şself had no bio┬şlog┬şi┬şcal descen┬şdants. Giv┬şen that, we can trace down a the┬şo┬şret┬şi┬şcal roy┬şal lin┬şeage start┬şing with his adopt┬şed son, born from his wife MarthaÔÇÖs first mar┬şriage; with the nephew he select┬şed as the pri┬şma┬şry heir of his estate; or with the senior-most heir of his father (own┬şer of the notion┬şal cher┬şry tree). Not that any of those major paths through the chart of Wash┬şing┬ştonÔÇÖs indi┬şrect descen┬şdants is nec┬şes┬şsar┬şi┬şly straight┬şfor┬şward either.
The whole mat┬şter seems at least as com┬şpli┬şcat┬şed as fig┬şur┬şing out who would be the Roman emper┬şor if Rome had nev┬şer fall┬şen, an exer┬şcise Bak┬şer works through in anoth┬şer Use┬şfulCharts video pre┬şvi┬şous┬şly fea┬ştured here on Open Cul┬şture. The pos┬şsi┬şbil┬şi┬şties for the Amer┬şi┬şcan monarch in 2026 come down to King Robert III, or Robert E. Lee V (and great-great grand┬şson of Robert E. Lee); Queen Bryn┬şda, or Bryn┬şda Hansen; King Richard, or Richard Wash┬şing┬şton; and King Lar┬şry II, or Lawrence Shaffn┬şer, the descen┬şdant of George Wash┬şing┬ştonÔÇÖs nephew Bushrod. Bak┬şer finds that Shaffn┬şer is the most con┬şvinc┬şing can┬şdi┬şdate for the job, which is hard to deny. Even apart from the rel┬şe┬şvant famil┬şial, polit┬şi┬şcal, and legal fac┬ştors, con┬şsid┬şer that name again. King Lar┬şry: apart from the title, how much more Amer┬şi┬şcan could it pos┬şsi┬şbly sound?
Relat┬şed con┬ştent:
Who Would Be Emper┬şor If the Roman Empire Still Exist┬şed Today?
How George Wash┬şing┬şton Became Pres┬şi┬şdent of the Unit┬şed States: It Was Weird┬şer Than You Think
George WashingtonÔÇÖs 110 Rules for Civil┬şi┬şty and Decent Behav┬şior
What We Can Learn from Past Pres┬şi┬şdents
A Japan┬şese Illus┬ştrat┬şed His┬şto┬şry of Amer┬şi┬şca (1861): Fea┬ştures George Wash┬şing┬şton Punch┬şing Tigers, John Adams Slay┬şing Snakes & Oth┬şer Fan┬ştas┬ştic Scenes
A Visu┬şal Time┬şline of World His┬şto┬şry: Watch the Rise & Fall of Civ┬şi┬şliza┬ştions Over 5,000 Years
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Brent SikkemaÔÇÖs Husband Convicted
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Also: Columbia UniversityÔÇÖs MFA show, a new Anni Albers biography, and Karla KnightÔÇÖs cosmic co
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Also: Columbia UniversityÔÇÖs MFA show, a new Anni Albers biography, and Karla KnightÔÇÖs cosmic codes.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            In January 2024, news about Brent Sikkema's brutal murder in Brazil shocked and horrified the art community. Last week, a federal jury found his estranged husband, Daniel Sikkema, guilty of arranging the murder. Our senior editor Valentina Di Liscia has the details of this grim story.Also today, a peek into the ideas brewing among the latest crop of MFA students at Columbia University, the secret life of Anni Albers, and Karla KnightÔÇÖs cosmic paintings. ÔÇöHakim Bishara, editor-in-chief

	
		
			Husband Found Guilty of Scheming Murder of Art Dealer Brent Sikkema
		
	
	
		A federal jury has found Daniel Sikkema guilty for his role in the murder-for-hire of his estranged husband, the New York art dealer Brent Sikkema. The 75-year-old gallerist was stabbed 18 times in his Rio de Janeiro townhouse in the early hours of January 14, 2024, in a brutal crime that shocked the art world and left SikkemaÔÇÖs loved ones searching for answers. | Valentina Di Liscia
	
	
		Read More
	

NewsUnionized staff at the Ohio State University's Wexner Center for the Arts officially called for the renaming of the institution and other campus buildings due to Les Wexner's ties to Jeffrey Epstein.Artist Alejandro ValenciaÔÇÖs work in the Columbia University MFA show is an┬áindictment of the schoolÔÇÖs crackdown on pro-Palestine activists, including a student who says she has been denied her degree.From Our Critics

	
		
			Anni Albers WasnÔÇÖt Afraid to Start From Zero
		
	
	
		You recognize Anni Albers from her revolutionary, abstract woven artworks, incisive essays, books, art prints, or fabric designs. But you might not know about the artistÔÇÖs obsession with white blouses, how much she delighted in English-language idioms, and her penchant for extra-crispy Kentucky Fried Chicken. Packed with lively detail and illuminating anecdotes, Nicholas Fox WeberÔÇÖs Anni Albers: A Life traces the historic sweep of the artistÔÇÖs biography and career, from her birth to a wealthy Jewish family in Berlin in 1899 to her 1933 escape from Nazi Germany to her later years in Connecticut. | Julie Schneider
	
	
		Read More
	


	
		
			Karla KnightÔÇÖs Cosmic Conspiracies
		
	
	
		What to make of such half-familiar signs? Are they richly composed blueprints for mysterious systems connected to extraterrestrial lifeforms ÔÇö schematics for advanced engines that could guide us toward wormholes to new coordinates of potential? Or attempts at mapping some underlying substrate of the universe? Such questions emerge from the shifting arrays in paintings and tapestries in Orbit. | Brian Karl
	
	
		Read More
	

Features


	
		
			A View From the Easel
		
	
	
		Kevin Callahan marks three years in his studio after losing his partner of 39 years and moving to a new home. He still finds reasons to smile as he paints and draws under the California sun.
	
	
		Read More
	

Memorable MomentFrom the Archive

	
		
			Anni AlbersÔÇÖs Thoughts on Textiles Loom Large
		
	
	
		On Weaving offers a model for how to write in a way that incorporates theoretical examination alongside practical content; in it Anni Albers provides valuable ÔÇö and often overlooked ÔÇö thoughts on art and creative work. | Becky Peterson
	
	
		Read More
	

                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Performance Cuts Through the Noise at the Venice Biennale
description:
Art Review
                Florentina Holzinger and Miet Warlop transform the Austrian and Belgian pavilions into immersive spec
body:
Art Review
                Florentina Holzinger and Miet Warlop transform the Austrian and Belgian pavilions into immersive spectacles of endurance, ecological dread, and controlled collapse.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Installation view of SEA WORLD VENICE, Florentina Holzinger's presentation at the Austrian pavilion of the 2026 Venice Biennale (all photos Eur├şdice Arratia/Hyperallergic)VENICE ÔÇö┬áUnless you were attending a silent retreat the past few weeks, you already know that no other Venice Biennale in recent history has gotten off to a more fraught start than this yearÔÇÖs. In May 2025, its artistic director,┬áKoyo Kouoh, passed away. Then there were canceled pavilions, boycotts protesting Israel and RussiaÔÇÖs participation, and the juryÔÇÖs resignation. KouohÔÇÖs summons to take a deep breath and exhale was, to say the least, a challenging request.In the midst of this upheaval, two national pavilions in the Giardini ÔÇö the Austrian and the Belgian ÔÇö slice straight through the outside rumbling with boldly experimental and imaginative performances that demanded sustained attention.Installation view of SEA WORLD VENICE, Florentina Holzinger's presentation at the Austrian pavilion of the 2026 Venice BiennaleThe choreographer and performance artist Florentina Holzinger┬átook over the Austrian pavilion with SEA WORLD VENICE, and she wanted anything but for you to close your eyes and shift into lower frequencies ÔÇö rather, she wants you to experience, with eyes wide open, the world in all its ecological doom. And she demands that you participate. Societal collapse has rarely been so enthralling.For years now, Holzinger has been a star of the European avant-garde theater world, creating epic spectacles with diverse, all-female casts that sometimes leave audience members (I count myself among them) gasping for air. Her mesmerizing stagecraft ÔÇö typically marked by queer feminist aesthetics and female nudity so hyper-present that it is no longer a source of mystification ÔÇö is fully on view in her Venice presentation. One woman is a clapper inside a huge bell, banging it on the hour with her torso. Other performers are suspended from harnesses like gravity-defying sculptures on an immense, slowly turning weathervane.Installation view of SEA WORLD VENICE, Florentina Holzinger's presentation at the Austrian pavilion of the 2026 Venice BiennaleIn SEA WORLD VENICE, Holzinger floods the pavilionÔÇÖs severe modern architecture with water and transforms it into a closed-loop circuit powered by our complicity: In the courtyard, visitors are invited to piss in two bright blue porta-potties flanking a massive water tank fed by the filtered urine, in which an impassive performer wearing only a scuba mask and mouthpiece remains submerged for hours at a time.Next door is a kind of sewage station where ÔÇťmaintenance workersÔÇŁ frantically try to manage out-of-control tubes, spraying the windows with a vile-looking brown fluid. Inside the flooded pavilion, another performer jet-skis in circles at full speed. It is an aquatic theme park gone awry ÔÇö presumably the result of our lack of care and greed ÔÇö and we are hopelessly submerged in it.Installation views of IT NEVER SSST, Miet WarlopÔÇÖs presentation at the Belgian pavilion of the 2026 Venice BiennaleA few hundred yards away,┬áMiet Warlop┬átransforms the Belgian pavilion into something between a construction site and a sports arena, with bleachers lined with plaster tiles etched with multilingual greetings and fragments of speech (ÔÇťSST,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťHOLA,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťOUI,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťSTOP,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťSALAMÔÇŁÔÇŽ).┬áIT NEVER SSST marks the first time Belgium has devoted its pavilion to performance. Throughout the opening hours, performers ÔÇö┬áWarlop's "living sculptures" ÔÇö frenetically darted up and down, hurling tiles between one another while ritualistically chanting, dancing, drumming, and stomping. Warlop has a background in visual arts; her signature is visually arresting tableaux vivants bristling with controlled chaos, with undercurrents of alienation and longing. She has carved out a distinctive place for herself in the experimental theater circuit, as seen in recent works such as┬áInhale Delirium Exhale, which I saw in Madrid last March, in which performers manipulated ÔÇö at times mastering, and at others struggling with ÔÇö 1,500 yards of silk.One of the most affecting performances in WarlopÔÇÖs pavilion is a solo by┬ácast member Alice Marchiori. She begins by spinning a record, and instantly, Barry WhiteÔÇÖs deep, velvety, albeit distorted, voice fills the space. Then, standing only an armÔÇÖs length from the audience, Marchiori painstakingly struggles to put on a constricting miniskirt cast in plaster ÔÇö Warlop calls it ÔÇťthe skirt inside the head,ÔÇŁ curator Caroline Dumalin told me ÔÇö her legs bearing bruises from the effort, before proceeding to climb the bleachers. The grueling sequence folds together WarlopÔÇÖs language of instability, endurance, and collapse.┬á┬á┬áMarchioriÔÇÖs mix of athleticism and almost ecstatic concentration brings the room to a standstill. During the seemingly endless climb, the constricting skirt progressively crumbles against her body. Then, finally, we feel a collective sense of cathartic release when her body rolls perilously down the bleachers, sending plaster tiles crashing to the floor on the way down. Sweaty and covered in plaster dust, she then rolls onto a table and simply lies there, breathing. We finally exhale.Installation views of IT NEVER SSST, Miet WarlopÔÇÖs presentation at the Belgian pavilion of the 2026 Venice BiennaleInstallation view of SEA WORLD VENICE, Florentina Holzinger's presentation at the Austrian pavilion of the 2026 Venice Biennale Installation views of SEA WORLD VENICE, Florentina Holzinger's presentation at the Austrian pavilion of the 2026 Venice Biennale Miet Warlop: IT NEVER SSST and Florentina Holzinger: Seaworld Venice continue at the Belgian pavilion and Austrian pavilion, respectively, at the 2026 Venice Biennale (Giardini della Biennale, Calle Giazzo, Venice, Italy) through November 22. The exhibitions were curated by Caroline Dumalin and Nora-Swantje Almes, respectively. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Venice Biennale
                      
                      
                        Belgium
                      
                      
                        Austria
                      
                      
                        Florentina Holzinger
                      
                      
                        Miet Warlop

title: How Betye Saar Set Black Dolls Free
description:
Features
                Nearing the occasion of her upcoming 100th birthday, an exhibition at the New York Historical celebrate
body:
Features
                Nearing the occasion of her upcoming 100th birthday, an exhibition at the New York Historical celebrates SaarÔÇÖs promised gift of her collection of dolls to the institution.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Betye Saar with Black dolls from her collection, 2025 (photo Tracye Saar-Cavanaugh, courtesy the artist and Roberts Projects, Los Angeles, California)Betye Saar has been accumulating ephemera ÔÇö taxidermied animals, cages, computer parts, and more ÔÇö throughout her life. Since the 1970s, she has crafted these found objects into assemblage artworks that often subvert racist artifacts and images, beginning with her famed sculpture ÔÇťThe Liberation of Aunt JemimaÔÇŁ (1972). ÔÇťI knew I could not avoid the pain, so it became part of my art,ÔÇŁ she said in 1973, as quoted in the monograph Black Doll Blues.┬áToday, her studio is filled to the brim with objects and ephemera gathered from nature, on her international travels, and at flea markets. On one special shelf sits her cherished collection of Black dolls. Some of these dolls, which Saar calls a ÔÇťfamily,ÔÇŁ have traveled to New York City, where they are now on view through October 4 at the New York Historical alongside paintings, prints, and sculptures featuring figurines. Nearing the occasion of her upcoming 100th birthday, the exhibition celebrates SaarÔÇÖs promised gift of her collection of over 100 dolls to the institution.Some of Saar's dolls in front of a photo of her shelf at home at the New York Historical (photo Jasmine Weber/Hyperallergic)While the artist, over the years, has considered cleaning out her studio, ÔÇťthe dolls are the one thing she can't discard,ÔÇŁ Wendy N─ülani E. Ikemoto, vice president and chief curator of the New York Historical and the exhibitionÔÇÖs co-curator, told Hyperallergic.ÔÇťIt is very meaningful that she's entrusting us with these dolls,ÔÇŁ added co-curator Rebecca Klassen.Born in 1926, Saar never had a Black doll of her own to play with as a girl. She came across her first as a college student in 1949 ÔÇö an Amosandra doll ÔÇö and became enthralled by them. She began incorporating them into her art practice in the 1970s, and they have figured into her work ever since, appearing in assemblages like ÔÇťIndigo MercyÔÇŁ (1975) and the print ÔÇťAunt Jemima and Hoo Doo DollÔÇŁ (1972)."I began collecting Black dolls decades ago. Some are hand-made, others manufactured, some even represent derogatory racial stereotypes. They each have a history and I feel some even have an energy of their past life, of the little child who loved them,ÔÇŁ Saar said in a statement to Hyperallergic.ÔÇťFemale Doll with Two Heads AboveÔÇŁ (2020), ÔÇťFemale Doll in Pink DressÔÇŁ (2020), and ÔÇťFemale Doll with Two HeadsÔÇŁ (2020) with a Janus doll and a ÔÇťtopsy-turvyÔÇŁ doll (photo Jasmine Weber/Hyperallergic)At the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the artist found comfort in these dolls and began pulling them down from the shelf and painting them into little scenes, using watercolors, a medium she had seldom explored over her long career.┬áÔÇťI took solace in painting them and imagining their mystical adventures,ÔÇŁ Saar continued.┬áDolls are ubiquitous playthings ÔÇö among the oldest toys in history. Simultaneously a form of textile art and an integral form of play, they also became, over time, an example of learned racism and a way to reinforce negative stereotypes through caricature. SaarÔÇÖs collection dates back to the 19th century, and includes many minstrel, mammy, golliwog, and ÔÇťtopsy-turvyÔÇŁ dolls, which sit alongside some of the earliest mass-produced Black dolls.┬áA mid-20th-century doll from Haiti, flanked by ÔÇťBlack Doll in a Mystic Sky (Green Skirt)ÔÇŁ (2021) and ÔÇťAunt Jemima and Hoo Doo DollÔÇŁ (1972) (photo Jasmine Weber/Hyperallergic)SaarÔÇÖs reimagining offered these figurines a new context and a life separate from ÔÇö or possibly in spite of ÔÇö their original intentions, transforming their meaning, alchemizing negative imagery into something potent, something positive.ÔÇťShe is really attracted to the energy behind the dolls and this idea that they've lived very rich past lives and that they have this almost embodied memory of an energetic charge within them,ÔÇŁ said Klassen.The exhibition at the New York Historical opens with the only doll made by SaarÔÇÖs hand, in 1974, ÔÇťHoo Doo Woman,ÔÇŁ informed by a Haitian doll from the mid-20th century in her collection that served as a rich source of inspiration from the 1970s until today.┬áBetye Saar, ÔÇť10 Dolls with Black MaskÔÇŁ (2022) (photo Paul Salveson, courtesy the artist and Roberts Projects, Los Angeles, California)In her watercolors and sculptures, Saar offers these dolls alternative lives beyond the parameters of their oft-derogatory origins. In her renderings of dolls drifting through celestial skies, Ikemoto explains that ÔÇťthere is a deeper cultural reference to the legend of the flying Africans who lift into the sky and fly away rather than submit to bondage.ÔÇŁ In another watercolor, the doll has become ÔÇťgrounded, and he is self-possessed,ÔÇŁ she adds.ÔÇťShe views the act of collecting and gathering and putting things together as a sort of ritual practice,ÔÇŁ Klassen said, ÔÇťfinding the energy within the object and then this ultimately transforming and then releasing the object onto the world.ÔÇŁBetye SaarÔÇÖs Black Dolls is on view at the New York Historical through October 4. (photo Jasmine Weber/Hyperallergic)
                  
                      
                        Features
                      
                      
                        Betye Saar
                      
                      
                        The New York Historical
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: How Betye Saar Set Black Dolls Free

title: For Ceija Stojka, Memory Is Survival
description:
Art Review
                 She rejected fascism not only by depicting what she endured in the Holocaust but also the tenderness
body:
Art Review
                 She rejected fascism not only by depicting what she endured in the Holocaust but also the tenderness of everyday Romani life.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Ceija Stojka, "Untitled" (1995), acrylic and sand on paper (┬ę Celia Pernot ┬ę 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/ Bildrecht, Vienna; all photos courtesy the Drawing Center unless otherwise noted)
    
        
            In an untitled landscape Ceija Stojka made in 1995, the sunset lights up an Austrian lake. Her familyÔÇÖs traveling wagon is on the water and her kin is around the perimeter gazing at the scenery and fishing. Thick brushstrokes of acrylic on paper emphasize the immediacy of the idyllic memory. Throughout her memoirs, including The Memoirs of Ceija Stojka, Child Survivor of the Romani Holocaust, posthumously translated in 2022, Stojka equates the Romani tradition of living in horse-drawn wagons with natural splendor ÔÇö sleeping outside, enjoying vistas, and hearing birds chirp ÔÇö manifesting the cultureÔÇÖs spirit of independence. Wagons themselves are a key symbol of sovereignty;┬áthe wheel even appears on the Romani flag.┬áOn view at the Drawing Center, Ceija Stojka: Making Visible showcases her self-taught practice and outsider perspective, both in the art world and as a Romani woman in Europe, through over 50 paintings and drawings. Like some of the best artists of the late 20th and early 21st centuries, Stojka, who passed away in 2013 at age 79, worked across media ÔÇö poetry, painting, writing, performance, and music. Her work is evidence that the core of creativity lies not in institutional education ÔÇö┬áshe attended formal schooling only briefly as a child ÔÇö┬ábut in the capacity to convey complex personal experience. As Roma writer and scholar Cristiana Grigore, a contributor for this magazine who was a consultant for the exhibition, told me, ÔÇťShe didnÔÇÖt need permission or validation that usually comes with being officially called an artist. She just had the desire to express herself with the instruments she had.ÔÇŁ┬á┬áAn Austrian Romani child survivor of the Holocaust, her art also made the atrocities inflicted on her people publicly visible, raising awareness of the richness of their culture as well as the centuries of persecution that persist today. In the late 1980s, she became the first Romani-Austrian woman to recount her experience of the Holocaust, though she initially faced resistance from her own historically patriarchal and insular community. Through her paintings, she rejected fascism not only by depicting what she endured but also by embracing freedom, underscored by tender examinations of everyday Romani life. What she accomplishes is more than a reminder to never forget history: It is a painstaking argument for the value of remembering, at both the personal and collective levels.Ceija Stojka, "Untitled" (1995), acrylic and sand on paper (┬ę Celia Pernot ┬ę 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/ Bildrecht, Vienna)In 1933, Stojka was born into a Lovara family, one of five ethnic Romani groups in Austria during the early 20th century; as a whole, they currently number approximately 10 to 12 million people across Europe with many diverse subgroups, customs, and languages. Across these varied backgrounds, a common thread is centuries of persecution stretching back to the Holy Roman Empire in the 1500s ÔÇö which continues despite the efforts of advocacy groups, whose work intensified in the aftermath of the Holocaust, which killed an estimated 500,000 Romani people.Damaging stereotypes accompany this systematic oppression, including a notion of ÔÇťgypsy lifeÔÇŁ as synonymous with exoticized mystery, vagrancy, and danger. Stojka and her family led a peripatetic life throughout Austria, a lifestyle that declined throughout the 20th century, but the richness of the experiences she affectionately documented in paintings and memoirs dismantles such tropes.In 1941, when Stojka was around eight years old, her father was arrested and taken to a camp in Dachau, where he was later murdered. She and the rest of her family were taken to Auschwitz two years later. Her sense of loss and confusion during this time is suggested by another untitled 1995 painting depicting the same body of water as the aforementioned painting, Lake Neusiedel, at the border between Austria and Hungary. But in this scene, set in winter, the lake is placid, and objects are scattered outside a cluster of abandoned caravans beside a Nazi flag buried in the snow, a clear reference to the Gestapo rounding up Romani.┬áCeija Stojka, "Untitled (They Devoured Us)" (1995), watercolor on paper (courtesy of Lorely French. Photo: Bill Carrigan. ┬ę 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / Bildrecht, ViennaThe artistÔÇÖs description of her two years across Auschwitz, Ravensbr├╝ck, and Bergen-Belsen as a child in her memoirs is devastating: the constant presence of death, incessant physical and verbal abuse, starvation, and unbearable labor. In one poem, Stojka writes, ÔÇťMy fear stayed in auschwitz / and in the camps. / Auschwitz is my overcoat / Bergen-Belsen is my dress / And Ravensbr├╝ck is my undershirt / What should I be afraid of?ÔÇŁ┬áHer drawings are the visual manifestation of her words. ÔÇťSad EarthÔÇŁ (1998) renders a mass arrival of prisoners to Auschwitz, bodies clustered together as a series of long green lines, suffering in the winter. Here and elsewhere, the rendering of figures loosely, almost abstractly, is a critical tool for the artist to emphasize the trauma of her experience.┬áOne of the most unsettling images of Stojka's oeuvre, the watercolor-on-paper work ÔÇťThey Devoured UsÔÇŁ (1995), frames the face of a Nazi officer with bloodshot eyes, his mouth gaping horrifically with blood spurting out of it. The painting embodies the term porajmos, coined by activists with the dual meaning of ÔÇťthe devouringÔÇŁ and ÔÇťthe destruction,ÔÇŁ to reference the Romani Holocaust.Ceija Stojka, "Untitled" (1999), acrylic on cardboard (┬ę 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / Bildrecht, Vienna; photo Matthias Reichelt)ÔÇťUntitledÔÇŁ (1999), meanwhile, layers emaciated, wraithlike bodies holding red bowls of soup. Their compressed bodies, brownish skin marked with black lines, and gaunt extremities expose the destitution of the camps. A small tree branch, which frequently accompanies StojkaÔÇÖs signature, grows from the lower right-hand corner of this composition, referencing a small deciduous tree that her family found at Bergen-Belsen and used for sustenance right before they were liberated. Visiting the camp back in the early 2000s, Stojka actually found and reunited with the tree that literally saved her family ÔÇö again, nature provided.While StojkaÔÇÖs artistic and advocacy work in the aftermath of the Holocaust certainly was critical, it is important to note that she also lived an expansive, vigorous life afterward. This included re-enrolling in school to achieve full literacy, raising three children, and negotiating a country that continued to hold deep prejudices against the Romani. In fact, Stojka only began painting late in life, while spending time with her grandchildren. They would do arts and crafts together; informal experimentation eventually turned into a full-time practice. ÔÇťI canÔÇÖt help but stop and think about the fact that she was inspired by her grandchildren and the tools that were available to them,ÔÇŁ Grigore reflected. ÔÇťShe had the freedom within herself, and the courage, to not say to herself ÔÇśIÔÇÖm too old to do this.ÔÇÖÔÇŁStojkaÔÇÖs visual and writing practices evidence the artist as a polymath, a maker capable of implementing different media to achieve a cohesive and massive body of work for us to interpret. But as Grigore puts it, ÔÇťShe has a very meaningful message to share with the world; the focus is on the message itself, and not on the medium, whether we call it storytelling, music, painting, or poetry.ÔÇŁ That message ÔÇö that art is essential to preserving both individual and collective memory in the service of better futures ÔÇö is evident in the expressive, enduring, and consequential works she left behind.Ceija Stojka: Making Visible continues at the Drawing Center (35 Wooster Street, Soho, Manhattan) through June 7. The exhibition was curated by Lynne Cooke with Noelig Le Roux.
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Ceija Stojka
                      
                      
                        The Drawing Center
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: For Ceija Stojka, Memory Is Survival

title: Ansel Adams Trust Slams Gallery for AI-Generated Work at AIPAD Photography Show
description: The Ansel Adams Publishing Rights Trust released a statement on Saturday slamming the recent decision by New YorkÔÇÖs Danziger Gallery to offer an AI-
body:
The Ansel Adams Publishing Rights Trust released a statement on Saturday slamming the recent decision by New YorkÔÇÖs Danziger Gallery to offer an AI-generated artwork referencing the famed photographerÔÇÖs work at the 2026 edition of the AIPAD Photography Show in April.

	The artwork, which still appears on DanzigerÔÇÖs website, does not contain a title but is headlined A.I. GENERATED, From the prompt: Make a realistic color version of Ansel AdamsÔÇÖ iconic ÔÇťMoonrise Over HernandezÔÇŁ. It is listed as printed by master printer Esteban Mauchi. Danziger offered the piece in its booth at the fairÔÇöwhich ran from April 22 to April 26ÔÇöalongside work by Seydou Ke├»ta, Hoda Afshar, and Matthew Porter, among others.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	In the statement, the trust said it ÔÇťdid not authorize, endorse, consent to, or acquiesceÔÇŁ to the work being exhibited or offered for sale and claimed that the piece ÔÇťexploited AnselÔÇÖs name, reputation, and his most iconic image, while failing to identify any human artist responsible for its creation.ÔÇŁ The trust said further that it was not notified by the gallery prior to its appearance and that, once it was alerted, it reached out to Danziger to have the work removed, which the gallery appears not to have done.

	ÔÇťAnsel was an innovator who expanded the expressive and technical possibilities of his medium. He was remarkably prescient aboutÔÇöand excited byÔÇöthe potential of computers to transform photography. The TrustÔÇÖs concerns are not about AI or creative experimentation in the abstract. This is fundamentally about artistsÔÇÖ rights and moral rightsÔÇöand respect for human dignity,ÔÇŁ the trust said, in a post on Instagram.

	Danziger, which was founded in 1989, did not return a request for comment from ARTnews at press time. However, on Monday,  founder James Danziger released a statement on the galleryÔÇÖs website claiming responsibilty for the creation of AI-generated image. Danziger defended his right to create the image, citing MoonriseÔÇśs public domain status, and said he did so ÔÇťwith great respect to the image and the artist. ÔÇŁ Further, he described the response at the fair as ÔÇťlargely positiveÔÇŁ with most negative comments ÔÇťdirecvted at AI.ÔÇŁ He claimed that AdamsÔÇÖs photo served as a ÔÇťstarting pointÔÇŁ for a ÔÇťtransformativeÔÇŁ image that involved ÔÇťextensive human intervention, editing, proofing, and refinement.ÔÇŁ
	

	ÔÇťAs┬áthe image is┬áin the public domain┬áI had every right to create a new┬áand transformative work,ÔÇŁ Danziger wrote. ÔÇťMy interest in doing this┬áwas based┬áon my love of┬áthe┬áiconic image, my interest┬áin seeing┬áhow A.I. could be used as a tool for creativity,┬áand to create an imagining of what Adams saw┬áin real life┬áas he was driving along US. Highway 84┬áthat made him stop his Pontiac station wagon and scramble to set up his bulky 8├Ś10 view camera as the sun was setting on┬áthe adobe church and cemetery┬ácrosses┬áwhile the moon appeared┬áthrough the clouds.ÔÇŁ

	Danziger closed his statement with a quote from Adams that he seemed to infer indicated that the photographer would have been okay with his alteration of the image.

	Several prominent figures in photography responded in the comments of the TrustÔÇÖs post to voice their displeasure with the situation. Longtime White House photographer Pete Souza wrote that DanzigerÔÇÖs decision to offer the image was ÔÇťmorally wrongÔÇŁ and ÔÇťendangers the rights of all photographers.ÔÇŁ Pulitzer PrizeÔÇôwinning photojournalist David Hume Kennerly wrote that he was a friend of Adams and posited that the photographer ÔÇťwould have hated this rip-off.ÔÇŁ

	This isnÔÇÖt the first time the trust has gotten into a dust-up over AI-generated images trading on AdamsÔÇÖs name. In 2024, the trust publicly denounced Adobe for including AI-generated images in its stock catalogue that referenced ÔÇťAnsel Adams-Style Photography.ÔÇŁ AdobeÔÇÖs official terms of use at the time banned users from uploading AI-generated images ÔÇťcreated using prompts containing other artist names, or created using prompts otherwise intended to copy another artist.ÔÇŁ After the trust took its dispute public, Adobe said it removed the content.
	

	EditorÔÇÖs Note, 5/25/2026: This article has been updated with a statement from gallery founder James Danziger.

schema.headline: Ansel Adams Trust Slams Gallery for AI-Generated Work at AIPAD Photography Show
